filer: Enerwise Global Technologies, LLC dba CPower
overall_summary: Load Flexibility Parties urge FERC to expand principles for large load interconnection to include load flexibility as a quickly deployable alternative to new generation. They stress that large loads using demand response should not receive preferential treatment, and that rules should maintain equity across all demand response customers in wholesale markets.

quotes.text: While new generation and transmission infrastructure will be part of the solution, other more quickly deployable solutions, such as load flexibility, will be necessary. Simply put, load flexibility helps balance supply and demand by reducing demand when called upon by the grid operator.
quotes.concern: Load flexibility as faster-deployable solution for demand balancing

quotes.text: The ANOPR proposes to allow for expedited interconnection of large loads that co-locate with generation. While this is likely to be an important part of the solution space, relying on new generation alone to meet increased demand will put reliability at risk in the intervening years that it takes for these generation assets to come online.
quotes.concern: Generation alone inadequate due to long build times; load flexibility needed

quotes.text: Given this reality, load flexibility – a more readily available resource - must also play a part in enabling the interconnection of large loads. Leveraging load flexibility has a couple of key advantages: it will be quicker to deploy, and it will be less expensive in most cases.
quotes.concern: Load flexibility key advantages: faster deployment and lower cost

quotes.text: Load flexibility can be deployed much more quickly than new generation because no interconnection process or lengthy construction period applies to load flexibility that uses an already installed behind the meter asset or does not use a behind the meter asset at all.
quotes.concern: Load flexibility avoids interconnection delays of generation assets

quotes.text: Load flexibility is inexpensive because it is often enabled just by putting in place simple metering and communication mechanisms, or in the case of automated demand response, it can be as straightforward as installing a programmable thermostat. In either case, these solutions are low cost and quickly achievable.
quotes.concern: Load flexibility cost-effective with simple implementation

quotes.text: All customers who provide load flexibility should be treated equitably regardless of whether they are participating in legacy ISO/RTO constructs or are providing this flexibility as a new large load customer. Equity should be provided across customer types in terms of payment, performance, order of dispatch, dispatch frequency, and duration of dispatch.
quotes.concern: Equity required across all demand response customer types

quotes.text: In short, large loads that agree to be curtailable in exchange for an expedited interconnection should, in no case, receive preferential treatment to other types of demand response in the wholesale markets. Such treatment would run afoul of the Federal Power Act, which prohibits wholesale market rates, terms, or conditions that constitute preferential treatment or that are unduly discriminatory.
quotes.concern: Large loads must not receive preferential treatment over existing DR

quotes.text: large loads that interconnect but do not bring their own 'capacity' in the form of generation or load flexibility must be first off the system when there is an impending reserve shortage.
quotes.concern: Non-capacity-bearing loads should have lowest dispatch priority

quotes.text: The Joint Parties suggest revising principles three, five, and seven as discussed below. Principle three states that 'to the extent practicable, load and hybrid facilities should be studied together with generating facilities'. This principle should be expanded to allow for study with other types of incremental resources, including load flexibility, in the appropriate location.
quotes.concern: Expand study principle to include load flexibility alongside generation

quotes.text: Principle five states that 'hybrid facilities should be studied based on the amount of injection and/or withdrawal rights requested'. The Joint Parties agree with this principle but ask that it be clarified that a hybrid facility would include not just load coupled with generation but also load coupled with any other appropriately located incremental resource, including flexible load.
quotes.concern: Clarify hybrid facilities to include load coupled with flexible load

quotes.text: Principle seven states that 'the interconnection study of large loads that agree to be curtailable and hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable should be expedited.' The Joint Parties agree with this principle but ask that it be expanded to state that large loads that agree to be curtailable or that arrange for load flexibility with an appropriately sited resource be entitled to an expedited interconnection process.
quotes.concern: Expand Principle 7 to cover loads arranged with nearby load flexibility

bins.name: Load flexibility faster alternative
bins.description: Load flexibility can be deployed more quickly than new generation, avoiding long construction timelines and interconnection delays. It provides immediate tools to balance supply and demand while generation assets are being built.

bins.name: Load flexibility cost efficiency
bins.description: Load flexibility is less expensive than new generation, using simple metering, communication, and thermostat modifications. Costs are low and achievable, making it an economical solution for demand balancing.

bins.name: Expand study to include flexibility
bins.description: Interconnection studies should analyze large loads in combination with multiple resource types, including load flexibility. This broader approach can accelerate interconnection by leveraging resources that deploy faster.

bins.name: Expedited process with flexibility
bins.description: Large loads that bring load flexibility should qualify for expedited interconnection. The filing expands the concept to include loads that arrange flexibility through appropriately sited resources, not just self-provided curtailment.

bins.name: Study loads with other resources
bins.description: Principle 3 should expand to allow joint study of large loads with load flexibility and other incremental resources. This enables faster assessment by combining resources that deploy at different timelines.

bins.name: Hybrid facilities with flexible load
bins.description: Clarify that hybrid facilities encompass load coupled with any appropriately located incremental resource, including flexible load. This broadens hybrid treatment to enable demand response capabilities.

bins.name: Preserve equity across all DR customers
bins.description: All demand response customers must be treated equally regardless of customer type. Large loads should not receive preferential treatment, dispatch priority, or better payment terms than existing demand response resources.

bins.name: Reserve shortage dispatch priority
bins.description: Large loads that do not bring generation or load flexibility capacity should be first off the system during reserve shortages. This protects existing demand response from increased dispatch frequency.
